# Discussion

Our hierarchical Beta-Binomial quantifies how mutation prevalence varies across cancer types within a gene. The gene-level concentration parameter $s_g$ provides a clear summary of cross-cancer mutation consistency, separating broadly-active tumor suppressors (TP53, $s \approx 3$) from
narrowly cancer-specific oncogenes (EGFR, $s \approx 19$). These patterns align with known biological behavior of these genes, suggesting that the model is capturing meaningful structure in the data.

The model further provides a useful predictive framework. The gene-level posterior predictive distribution can be used to estimate mutation prevalence in a new or less-studied cancer type. This may be helpful for guiding expectations in small datasets or early-stage clinical studies. 

We have two main methodological takeaways. First, while HMC provides reliable posterior inference, mean-field ADVI offers a much faster approximation that performed similarly to HMC. However, full-rank ADVI did not align well with HMC, despite being more flexible. This suggests that increased model flexibility does not always lead to better approximations.

Overall, this analysis demonstrates how hierarchical Bayesian modeling can be used to estimate mutation prevalence from real-world cancer data. By balancing infomration sharing across cancer types with gene-specific differences, the model provides improved estimation and summaries of cross-cancer mutation behaviour. 

## Limitations 
Our model pools information within gene, but does not extend this structure across genes. A second
hierarchical level ($\mu_g \sim \text{Beta}(A, B)$ with hyper-hyperpriors) is
natural but borderline-identifiable with only 10 genes. We also treat cancer types as independent, ignoring the tissue-of-origin structure that has been shown to be a dominant source of molecular variation in pan-cancer analyses [@hoadley2018cell]. In addition, mutation status is treated as binary which may over-simplify differences between mutation types and locations (e.g. *KRAS* G12C vs G12D). Finally, for simplicity, our model assumes independence across genes and therefore does not capture known patterns of co-occurrence and mutual exclusivity in cancer genomics, which have been linked to underlying pathway interactions [@eltekle2021cooccurrence; @unni2015egfrkras], and can be modeled using more complex approaches such as Bayesian networks [@kuipers2018mutational]. These limitations suggest clear directions for further developing this analysis.

## Team contributions {-}

This project was done collaboratively, with idea generation, model selection, coding, literature reviewing, and writing split evenly between us. Due to some technical difficulties and a lot of in-person work, most of the commits on the Github repository are by Annmarie, however, this is not representative of the division of work and was just for convenience.
